hook: The tin-roofed College Park bungalow where Jack Kerouac lived in 1957-58, now a working writers' residency.
facts: Kerouac and his mother shared a two-room apartment at the rear of this c.1920 cottage at 1418 1/2 Clouser Avenue from July 1957 until spring 1958.
He lived here when On the Road was published (September 1957) and reportedly typed the original scroll manuscript of The Dharma Bums while in the house.
Since the late 1990s the house has been owned by The Kerouac Project of Orlando, a nonprofit that runs roughly four three-month writer residencies per year.
It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2013 (NRHP reference number 12001254).
A Florida historical marker was erected at the site in 2014 by the Kerouac Project and the Florida Department of State.

The cottage sits at the intersection of Clouser Avenue and Shady Lane Drive; it is a private residence for the writer-in-residence and is not a walk-in museum (exterior viewing only outside scheduled public events).
w: In this modest College Park bungalow Jack Kerouac lived with his mother in 1957, the year 'On the Road' made him famous, and typed 'The Dharma Bums' here in an eleven-day burst. The little house now hosts writers on residency, keeping the Beat flame in suburban Florida. Exterior viewing only. Where the road came briefly to rest.

hook: A free 9,000-sq-ft museum tucked behind a working family knife factory that has hand-forged blades since 1938.
facts: Randall Made Knives was founded in Orlando in 1938 by Walter Doane 'Bo' Randall Jr. and is still a family-run maker of hand-finished knives.
The museum sits at the factory and showroom at 4857 South Orange Blossom Trail, Orlando, FL 32839.
It displays thousands of Randall knives past and present plus blades from many other makers that Bo Randall collected during his life, alongside vintage articles and wartime letters from servicemen.
Admission is free, but visitors must check in at the showroom first and are then directed to the separate museum building.
The museum is open Monday through Thursday, 10am to 4pm, and closed Friday through Sunday.
w: Behind a working knife factory hides a free museum of blades: thousands of knives from around the world, fighting daggers and a giant working switchblade you could not pocket. The Randall family has forged knives by hand since 1938, for soldiers and astronauts. Steel, by the thousand.

n: The Disney Collection at the Orlando Public Library
hook: A Disney park-history archive hidden on the fourth floor of the downtown public library, started before the Magic Kingdom even opened.
facts: The collection is housed in Learning Central on the fourth floor of the Orlando Public Library at 101 E Central Blvd, Orlando, FL 32801.
The library began amassing the archive in the 1960s, after Disney announced plans to build what became Walt Disney World.
Holdings include more than 200 books, around 75 periodicals, and hundreds of pieces of memorabilia, plus photographs, audiovisual materials and artwork.
A chronological depository of Disney press clippings runs back to 1971 and includes nationwide newspaper clippings and radio and television logging reports.
Because of the rarity of the items, the collection is non-circulating: materials are on reserve and can only be used on library property.
w: On a quiet upper floor of the downtown library sits one of the deepest public archives of Disney park history: ride blueprints, souvenir guides and ephemera, gathered since the 1960s. Not a gift shop, just shelves of the real paper trail behind the magic. Read it on site, by appointment. Disney, off the clock and in the stacks.

hook: A 12-foot solar-lit aluminum globe whose blue panels are printed with native shoal bass that seem to swim laps as the LEDs shift.
facts: Created by sculptor Deedee Morrison, the 12-foot globe stands in the plaza in front of the Orange County Regional History Center, at Central Boulevard and Magnolia Avenue (65 E Central Blvd).

The cobalt-blue aluminum panels are screen-printed with a school of shoal bass native to Florida; the fish images were made by the Japanese gyotaku technique of inking a real fish and pressing it onto paper.
Internal solar-powered LED lights shift in patterns that create the optical illusion of the fish circling an aquarium.
It was the first of the works installed under the See Art Orlando project, a permanent downtown public-art collection unveiled by Mayor Buddy Dyer on November 18, 2013.
The piece is meant to prompt reflection on sustainability and people's role in the natural ecosystem; sources through late 2025 confirm it remains in place and on public view.
w: A glowing steel orb in a downtown plaza holds a school of Florida shoal bass that seem to swim through its perforated skin, an optical trick of layered, fish-printed panels. Deedee Morrison made it for the See Art Orlando project in 2013, lit from within after dark. Circle it and the fish appear to move. Public art that swims in place.

hook: A three-story, 19,000-sq-ft kitsch McDonald's near International Drive with an arcade, pizza station and a 30-foot Ronald out front.
facts: Located at 6875 Sand Lake Road, Orlando, FL 32819, near the International Drive / Sand Lake Road intersection and ICON Park.
At about 19,000 square feet it is one of the largest McDonald's in the world and is nicknamed 'Epic McD.'
The original restaurant opened in 1976; the building was closed in 2015, demolished, and a new one reopened on the site in 2016.
It carries an expanded menu beyond standard McDonald's fare, including brick-oven pizza, Belgian waffles and made-to-order pasta.
Features include an arcade with over 100 games and a multi-story themed PlayPlace, and a roughly 30-foot Ronald McDonald image at the entrance.
The restaurant operates 24 hours a day, 365 days a year.
w: Not just a McDonald's but a two-storey playhouse of one, the self-proclaimed world's largest, off International Drive. Beyond the burgers wait a giant arcade, a gelato-and-waffle bistro and pure 1970s roadside excess. Tacky, huge and oddly joyful. Eat your fries beneath the golden arches, supersized into spectacle.

hook: A regional history museum inside a restored 1927 Beaux-Arts courthouse whose old courtrooms once hosted Ted Bundy's trial.
facts: The museum occupies the restored former Orange County Courthouse at 65 E Central Blvd, Orlando, FL 32801, a Beaux-Arts building constructed in 1927.
The History Center opened in the restored courthouse in 2000.

Serial killer Ted Bundy was tried in Orlando for the 1978 murder of Kimberly Leach beginning in January 1980; the trial was held in the courthouse complex (in an adjoining annex that was later demolished, not the surviving 1927 building).
A preserved 1927-era courtroom (Courtroom B) is part of the museum, and one front bench bears a carved 'Ted Bundy' inscription whose authenticity is disputed.
When built, the courthouse's top floor held the county jail, with a jailer's quarters, an infirmary and segregated cellblocks.
legend: The building is a frequent stop on Orlando ghost tours, with visitors and staff reporting unexplained sounds and a reputedly haunted atmosphere; these hauntings are folklore, not established fact.
w: Central Florida's story fills a handsome 1927 Beaux-Arts courthouse downtown, from Timucua artifacts to citrus and theme parks. In an adjoining annex, since demolished, Ted Bundy stood trial in 1979. Quiet rumours of a haunting persist. Climb the marble stairs through four floors of the past. History, in the old halls of justice.

hook: A Spiritualist community of mediums chartered in 1894; its Andrew Jackson Davis bookstore is the camp welcome center.
facts: The Southern Cassadaga Spiritualist Camp Meeting Association was formally chartered on December 18, 1894.
It was founded by trance medium George P. Colby, who said spirit guides led him to the site.

The Andrew Jackson Davis Building, the camp bookstore and welcome center, stands at 1112 Stevens St and is one of the town's oldest buildings; it was named for the Spiritualist writer Andrew Jackson Davis in March 1976.
The camp is listed on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places as the Cassadaga Historic District.
The bookstore keeps the camp's list of certified mediums available for walk-in readings and is the largest area source of books on Spiritualism.
w: A whole town of mediums, founded in 1894 by a Spiritualist led, he said, by spirit guides to this piney Florida hollow. Cassadaga remains a living camp of certified psychics, healing services and a welcome-center bookstore. Book a reading, or just feel the strangeness. America's oldest Spiritualist community, still in session.

hook: A 1927 Mediterranean Revival hotel where resident mediums still give readings, a few doors from the Spiritualist camp.
facts: The current Cassadaga Hotel was built in 1927 (completed 1928, opened to guests November 28, 1928) after the earlier 1901 wooden hotel burned on Christmas Eve 1926.
It was designed by architect William J. Carpenter, AIA, in the Mediterranean / Spanish Revival style.
Originally owned by the National Spiritualist Association, it was sold to private owners after camp founder George Colby's death in 1933 and is no longer part of the official camp.
It sits at 355 Cassadaga Rd, Lake Helen, FL 32744, roughly 350 metres from the camp's Andrew Jackson Davis bookstore.
The building houses Sinatra's Ristorante and resident psychics and mediums who give daily readings to visitors.
legend: The hotel is widely reported as haunted, with guests describing a cigar-smoking spirit nicknamed 'Arthur' and other apparitions on the ghost-tour circuit.
w: The 1927 hotel across the lane from the Spiritualist camp leans hard into the town's mystique: resident mediums read cards in the lobby, and the upstairs rooms come with their own ghost stories. Faded, incense-scented and frozen in time. Stay the night if you dare. A haunted hotel that markets the haunting.

hook: A legendary quarter-mile of Interstate 4 near the St Johns River bridge said to be paved over a forgotten 1880s graveyard.
facts: Interstate 4 was built across this stretch near Sanford in 1959-1960, crossing the St Johns River at the mouth of Lake Monroe.
The St Johns River / Lake Monroe crossing here is carried by the St Johns River Veterans Memorial Bridge, connecting Sanford in Seminole County with DeBary in Volusia County.
The Florida Department of Transportation has stated that crash numbers on this section of I-4 fall within a normal range, not unusually high.
The area's history traces to St Joseph's, a small German Catholic colony established near Sanford in the 1880s that was struck by a yellow-fever outbreak in 1887.
legend: Local lore holds that four members of a German family who died of yellow fever in 1887 were buried here and that their graves were paved over when I-4 was built, located beneath the eastbound lanes just south of the river bridge.
Drivers report radio and CB interference, floating orbs, phantom trucks and roadside apparitions along the 'Dead Zone'.
Hurricane Donna is said to have crossed directly over the gravesite on September 10, 1960, shortly after the road opened.
w: A quarter-mile of Interstate 4 by the St Johns River carries one of Florida's most stubborn legends: that the road was paved over a tiny yellow-fever graveyard whose graves now curse the asphalt with wrecks and strange calls. Highway officials note the crash rate is normal. Cross it and decide. Florida's most haunted commute.

hook: Orlando's oldest cemetery, founded 1880, full of Victorian monuments, veterans' sections and ghost-tour lore.
facts: Eight Orlando residents pooled funds to buy 26 acres and form the cemetery in 1880; it was renamed Greenwood Cemetery in 1915 at the founders' request.
It has grown to roughly 86 acres and is the City of Orlando's municipal cemetery, located at 1603 Greenwood St, Orlando, FL 32801.
It contains dedicated sections for Confederate, Union, Spanish-American War, World War I and World War II veterans.
July Perry, lynched in 1920 after attempting to vote in the Ocoee election-day violence, is buried here and received a marked headstone in 2002.
The cemetery's sexton leads regular moonlight walking tours of the grounds and its history.
legend: Greenwood is a fixture of Orlando ghost lore, with visitors reporting apparitions among its Victorian monuments and mausoleums.
w: Orlando's oldest cemetery, laid out in 1880 and now a leafy 100-acre sprawl of Victorian angels and mayors' tombs. The city runs lantern-lit history walks among the live oaks after dark. Pioneers, sheriffs and a circus performer or two rest here. Come for the monuments, the moss and the moonlit lore.

hook: Orlando's signature downtown park, ringed by a lake with swan boats and the fountain that serves as the city's emblem.
facts: The Linton E. Allen Memorial Fountain at the lake's centre was installed in 1957 and is an emblem of Orlando; it was refurbished after a 2009 lightning strike.
The City of Orlando rents pedal-powered swan boats from a dock on the north end of the lake.
The park is centred on a 0.43-square-mile sinkhole lake in the Central Business District, with a paved perimeter path popular for walking.
The park's main address is 512 E Washington St, Orlando, FL 32801.
legend: Lake Eola's best-known haunting is the 'Ghost Dog,' a friendly brown terrier reported for decades playing along the eastern shore before vanishing.
One tragic version of the tale claims the dog was drowned in the lake by its owner's father; no news account corroborates it.
w: The green heart of downtown wraps a sinkhole lake, ringed by a footpath, paddled by swan-shaped pedal boats and centred on a glowing fountain. By day it's families and food trucks; by night, some swear, a 'Ghost Dog' pads the shore. The skyline reflects in the water at dusk. Orlando's living room, with a side of legend.

hook: An 1886 red-brick hardware building on Church Street, one of Orlando's first masonry buildings, now a restaurant and venue block.
facts: Joseph Bumby completed the structure in 1886 and ran a hardware business that remained a family operation until it closed in 1966.
Built of red brick in the late-Victorian commercial style, it was one of Orlando's first non-wooden buildings.
It stands at 110 W Church St (also addressed 102 W Church St) in downtown Orlando and is marked by a Bumby Hardware historical marker.
Hamburger Mary's occupied the building for 16 years and closed there on June 2, 2024; the building (part of the Bumby Arcade block) remains standing.
Bumby Avenue in Orlando is named for the same Joseph Bumby.
legend: The building is a stop on Orlando ghost tours, associated with a Victorian-era 'ghost girl' said to haunt the former hardware store.
w: A handsome 1886 brick block on Church Street, built as the Bumby Hardware company in Orlando's railroad-era downtown. Restaurants have come and gone — Hamburger Mary's left in 2024 — but the building endures, and so does its ghost: a Victorian girl said to wander the upper floor. Old Orlando, still standing.

hook: An 11-story 1923 Orange Avenue hotel, once Orlando's grandest, now converted to offices and ground-floor bars.
facts: The Angebilt was built from 1921 to 1923 and opened March 14, 1923, with 250 rooms on 11 floors.
It was designed by architect Murray S. King and stands at 37 N Orange Ave, Orlando.
The building was renovated into offices through the 1980s and served as an Orange County Courthouse annex starting in 1988.
Its ground floor now houses bars/restaurants (The Bellhop and Parlay) while upper floors remain offices.
legend: The hotel is a ghost-tour stop with reports of cold spots, phantom footsteps and the disembodied screams of a woman in its halls.
Lore ties the activity to an underground tunnel that once connected the building to the courthouse and jail.
w: When it opened on Orange Avenue in 1923, the eleven-storey Angebilt reigned as Orlando's grandest hotel, dreamed up by developer Joseph Angel. The guests are long gone — it holds offices now — but the ghost stories stayed, of screams on the upper floors. Look up at the facade. Faded jazz-age glamour, downtown.

hook: The 1889 Victorian train depot at the heart of Church Street Station, now a SunRail stop and ghost-tour landmark.
facts: The depot was built in 1889 by the South Florida Railroad for railroad magnate Henry B. Plant for about $18,000 by Sanford contractor T.B. Cotter, opening in January 1890.
Designed in the Eclectic Victorian / Shingle style, it was added to the U.S. National Register of Historic Places on April 22, 1976 (ref. 76000604) as the 'Old Orlando Railroad Depot.'
It served several railroads until 1926, when passenger service moved to the present Orlando Health/Amtrak station.
The restored depot is now a stop on the SunRail commuter line and anchors the Church Street Station entertainment district; its address is 4 Depot Place / 78 W Church St.
legend: The depot and the surrounding Church Street Station area (including the former Strand Hotel) feature on Orlando's downtown ghost-tour circuit.
w: A trim 1889 train depot, the oldest surviving in Orlando, marks where the railroad first brought citrus money and travellers to a frontier town. Restored in the Church Street Station district, it anchors the city's ghost-tour circuit. Trains still rumble past. Where Orlando arrived, one boxcar at a time.

n: Wells'Built Museum of African American History & Culture
hook: A Jim Crow-era Black hotel on the chitlin' circuit — host to Jackie Robinson, Ella Fitzgerald and Ray Charles — reborn as a museum in Orlando's Parramore district.
facts: Built by Dr. William Monroe Wells, an African-American physician, who opened it as a hotel for Black travelers barred from Florida's segregated hotels; he also built the adjacent South Street Casino as an entertainment venue.

As a stop on the 'chitlin' circuit' it lodged Black performers and figures who could not stay in white hotels, including Ella Fitzgerald, Count Basie, Ray Charles, B.B. King, Jackie Robinson and Thurgood Marshall.
Added to the National Register of Historic Places on February 4, 2000 (reference no. 00000006).
The former hotel was converted by the Association to Preserve African-American History and opened as the Wells'Built Museum of African American History and Culture in June 2009.
Located at 511 W South St in the historic Parramore district; it houses memorabilia of Orlando's African-American community plus Civil Rights-era displays and African art.
Open Monday-Friday 9am-5pm, plus the second weekend of each month (Saturday and Sunday).
w: Dr William Wells built this Parramore hotel so Black travellers and performers had somewhere to stay — and its rooms hosted Jackie Robinson, Ella Fitzgerald and Ray Charles on the chitlin' circuit. Now a museum of Black Orlando's history, it keeps that story alive. Where the greats slept when they could not elsewhere.

n: Zora Neale Hurston National Museum of Fine Arts
hook: "The Hurston" — Eatonville's museum to the author and folklorist, showcasing art of the African diaspora in the oldest Black-incorporated town in America.
facts: Founded in 1990 and operated by the Association to Preserve the Eatonville Community, Inc. (P.E.C.), honoring author and folklorist Zora Neale Hurston, who grew up in Eatonville.
Its exhibitions feature artworks of African-American artists and other artists of the African diaspora, with quarterly shows spotlighting emerging artists.
The museum is the year-round home of the annual ZORA! Festival celebrating Eatonville's history, culture and arts.
Located at 227 E Kennedy Blvd; it anchors the Zora Neale Hurston Trail of 16 historic markers around the town.
Eatonville, incorporated in 1887, is the oldest continuously incorporated Black municipality in the United States.
w: A small museum in Eatonville honours the town's most famous daughter, Zora Neale Hurston — author of 'Their Eyes Were Watching God' and a Harlem Renaissance star who grew up here in America's oldest Black town. It shows art by artists of African descent and anchors the annual ZORA! Festival. Eatonville's literary pride.

hook: The second-oldest house in America's oldest Black town — an 1888 wood-frame cottage Zora Neale Hurston used to visit, now a museum of early Eatonville life.
facts: Built in 1888, it is the second-oldest structure in Eatonville and one of two surviving examples of the town's pre-1900 single-story wood-frame dwellings.
Owned by Jim and Matilda Clark Moseley; Matilda was the niece of Eatonville's founder and first mayor, and author Zora Neale Hurston was a friend who often visited the house.
Restored and opened as a museum in 2000, it exhibits early Eatonville memorabilia.
Located at 11 Taylor Avenue and maintained by the Zeta Phi Beta Sorority's Zeta Educational Thespian Association and Florida State Leadership Conference.
w: This plain 1888 cottage ranks as the second-oldest building in Eatonville and now houses a little museum of the town Zora Neale Hurston made famous. Inside wait artifacts of one of America's first self-governing Black communities, incorporated in 1887. The everyday past of an extraordinary town.

hook: Older than the town it stands in — the African Methodist Episcopal congregation founded in 1881 on land donated by Maitland's Lewis Lawrence, six years before Eatonville was incorporated.
facts: Founded in 1881, six years before Eatonville's 1887 incorporation, making the congregation older than the town itself and one of the oldest African-American churches in Central Florida.
Named in honor of Lewis Lawrence of Maitland, who donated the land for the church.
The present church building, completed in 1974, stands on the original church land at the corner of E Kennedy Blvd and Taylor Avenue.
Located on E Kennedy Blvd a few doors from the Moseley House; the two share a combined Eatonville historical marker.
w: Founded in 1881, six years before the town itself, this AME congregation stands as a cornerstone of Eatonville's story of Black self-government. Generations have worshipped and organised within its walls; the brick sanctuary dates from 1974. A church older than the town around it. Faith that helped build a community.

hook: Orlando's oldest African-American neighborhood — a thriving Black enclave of theaters, shops and hotels in segregated Orlando, later cut apart by Interstate 4 and SR 408.
facts: Developed in the 1880s by James B. Parramore, Orlando's fourteenth mayor and a former Confederate soldier, as a segregated district to house Black workers employed in white Orlandoans' households.
Over roughly 60 years it grew into a thriving Black community of around 18,000 residents with schools, theaters, shops, restaurants and hotels.
The Holden-Parramore Historic District is recognized as Orlando's African-American commercial historic district, tied to the area's Black heritage.
In 1957 Interstate 4 was driven through the area and in the 1970s SR 408 further split it, destroying roughly 1,000 homes, around 200 businesses and several African-American churches.
Notable institutions include the Booker T. Washington Library (opened 1924) and the Wells'Built Museum on S Parramore Ave / W South St.
w: West of the tracks lies Parramore, Orlando's oldest Black neighbourhood, a once-thriving district of Black businesses, churches and the Wells'Built Hotel. Two highways carved through its heart last century, but its heritage endures. Walk its streets with the history in mind. The soul of Black Orlando, scarred but standing.

hook: A walkable district of brick-lined streets and early-20th-century bungalows just east of Lake Eola, built when Orlando's cattle ranchers and citrus growers put their fortunes into stately homes.
facts: Lies one block east of Lake Eola Park, bounded roughly by Summerlin Ave, E Robinson St, N Brown Ave, E Central Blvd and S Hyer Ave down to SR 408.
Blossomed in the first half of the 20th century as prosperous cattle ranchers and citrus growers invested in stately homes plus brick-lined streets and upscale community improvements.
Its housing stock is largely 1920s-1950s bungalows and cottages on small, shaded lots, known for cobblestone/brick streets and ancient oaks.

Falls within the city-recognized Lake Lawsona historic district, which encompasses both the Lake Lawsona-Fern Creek and Thornton Park areas with Craftsman, Mediterranean Revival and Colonial Revival homes.
Adjacent Lake Eola Park is the district's defining amenity — a 43-acre urban park with a weekly farmers market, swan boats and an event amphitheater.
w: East of Lake Eola spreads Thornton Park, a walkable pocket of brick streets, bungalows and oak-shaded sidewalks with downtown's most-loved restaurants and bars. One of the city's first suburbs, it kept its early-1900s charm as the towers rose nearby. Stroll the canopy, graze the patios. Old-Orlando soul, a block from the lake.

hook: Nearly 50 acres of botanical garden wrapped around an 1880s farmhouse, with one of the largest camellia collections in the Eastern U.S.
facts: The gardens cover nearly 50 acres of landscaped grounds and lakes at 1920 North Forest Avenue in the Audubon Park area of Orlando.
At the heart of the gardens is the Leu House Museum, the restored 19th-century home of Harry P. and Mary Jane Leu, on the National Register of Historic Places.
A 15-acre section, the Mizell-Leu House Historic District, was designated a historic district on December 29, 1994.
Harry P. Leu and his wife bought the property in 1936 and donated it to the City of Orlando in 1961.
The gardens hold one of the largest documented camellia collections in eastern North America and a formal rose garden.
w: Fifty acres of camellias, roses, palms and one of the South's largest formal rose gardens, all begun by Harry and Mary Jane Leu, an Orlando couple who gathered plants worldwide for decades. At its centre stands their 1880s lakeside home, now a museum. Wander the camellia woods. A lifelong obsession, gone public.

hook: A 205-foot pink-and-grey marble 'Singing Tower' carillon crowning one of peninsular Florida's highest points, ringing out over Edward Bok's landscape garden.
facts: The Singing Tower stands 205 feet (62 m) tall and houses a 60-bell carillon cast by John Taylor & Co. of Loughborough, England.
The tower sits atop Iron Mountain, one of the highest points of peninsular Florida at roughly 295 feet above sea level.
The gardens were designed by landscape architect Frederick Law Olmsted Jr. and dedicated in 1929 by President Calvin Coolidge.
It was the gift of Dutch-American author and Ladies' Home Journal editor Edward W. Bok.
Bok Tower Gardens is open daily and charges an admission fee; carillon recitals are played daily.
w: A 205-foot pink-marble bell tower crowns one of Florida's highest points, ringing out carillon concerts over gardens by Olmsted's son. Dutch-born publisher Edward Bok built it in 1929 as a gift to the American people, and lies buried at its foot. Climb Iron Mountain for the music. A singing tower in the citrus country.

hook: A lakeside grove of towering cypress and azaleas on Lake Maitland, anchored by the 'Exedra' — a curved stone monument framing the water.
facts: The roughly 5-acre public garden sits on the shore of Lake Maitland at 1365 Alabama Drive in Winter Park, open daily from 8 a.m. until dusk.
It contains the Exedra, a curved stone monument near the water's edge that is a popular photo and wedding backdrop.
The garden was developed in 1938; nurseryman Martin Daetwyler was hired to design the azalea plantings.
Enormous bald cypress trees shade the lakefront grounds.
Peak azalea bloom is typically January through March.
w: A hidden lakeside garden in Winter Park where azaleas blaze in late winter beneath ancient cypress, and a marble 'Exedra' of white columns frames the water. Quiet, free and beloved of wedding photographers, it keeps an old-Florida romance. Come at sunset over Lake Maitland. A secret garden, columns and all.

hook: A lush one-acre urban garden bar and event yard tucked into College Park, strung with lights for weddings, markets and evening drinks.
facts: The Acre is a one-acre rustic urban garden and gathering space at 4421 Edgewater Drive in Orlando's historic College Park neighborhood.
It operates as a private event venue and outdoor bar, hosting weddings, private parties, photo and small film shoots.
As of June 2026 it is open to the public on a limited schedule, generally Thursday through Sunday evenings (closed Monday through Wednesday).
The grounds are landscaped as an outdoor oasis a short distance from downtown Orlando.
w: Tucked behind a fence in College Park, the Acre turns a couple of green acres into a lantern-strung garden bar of picnic tables, fire pits and food trucks under the oaks. Open only a few evenings a week, it draws a young crowd to its leafy, fairy-lit calm. Bring a blanket. A backyard hangout the whole city wants in on.

hook: A 47-acre pocket of wild Florida in the middle of Winter Park, threaded with boardwalks, a butterfly garden and a spring-fed creek.
facts: Mead Botanical Garden covers about 47.6 acres at 1300 South Denning Drive in Winter Park and is free to enter.
Amenities include a butterfly garden, boardwalk, amphitheater, greenhouse and a creek running through several ecosystems.
The garden was established in 1940 from roughly 50 acres of upland hammock and wetlands.
It is a designated stop on the Great Florida Birding Trail.
The garden is named for botanist Theodore Luqueer Mead, an orchid and Amaryllis breeder.
w: Forty-seven acres of wild and cultivated green fold into Winter Park: a cypress boardwalk over a creek, a butterfly garden and trails where herons stalk the wetland. Named for a pioneering botanist, Mead stays free and low-key, a refuge from the theme-park bustle. Walk the boardwalk at dawn. Nature, hidden in the suburbs.

hook: A 440-acre wedge of protected pine flatwoods and bayhead swamp near Lake Tibet, with boardwalks and a nature center minutes from the theme parks.
facts: The preserve protects roughly 440 acres at 8777 Winter Garden-Vineland Road in southwest Orange County, near Lake Tibet.
It is managed by Orange County Parks and Recreation and is free to visit, open daily.
Trails total about 3.6 miles, including boardwalks over a tannic-water bayhead swamp.
The Vera Carter Environmental Center serves as the preserve's nature center and trailhead, with restrooms and a butterfly garden.
Habitats include sand pine scrub, pine flatwoods and cypress wetlands supporting eagles, gopher tortoises and alligators.
w: Just minutes from the theme parks but a world away, this 440-acre preserve protects pine flatwoods and cypress swamp on the shore of Lake Tibet. Miles of trail and boardwalk thread it, with a nature centre and the chance of deer, tortoises and bald eagles. Quiet, free and uncrowded. Real Florida, next door to the magic.

hook: A crystal-clear, 68-degree spring run that doubles as a natural lazy river — float it on an inner tube through shaded subtropical woods.
facts: Rock Springs flows through Kelly Park at 400 East Kelly Park Road in Apopka, roughly 20 miles northwest of Orlando.
The spring discharges about 26,000 gallons per minute at a constant 68-72 F year-round.
The tubing run is about three-quarters of a mile and takes 25-30 minutes to float.
The park opens at 8 a.m. and routinely reaches capacity within an hour or two on busy weekends; a capacity hotline is provided.
Kelly Park is operated by Orange County Parks and charges a per-vehicle entry fee.
w: A crystal-clear spring boils up from the ground at Kelly Park near Apopka and runs in a cool channel perfect for drifting on an inner tube. The water holds a steady 68 degrees year-round, dead clear over white sand, and the park fills early on hot weekends. Old-Florida swimming, from before the theme parks existed.

hook: Orlando's indie 'Antique Row' along Orange Avenue by Lake Ivanhoe — a lakeside Main Street district of vintage shops, breweries and the art museum.
facts: Ivanhoe Village is an accredited Orlando Main Street district on the northern edge of downtown, centered on Orange Avenue beside Lake Ivanhoe.
Its lakefront shopping strip was long known locally as Antique Row, lined with antique shops and vintage boutiques.
The district is anchored by the Orlando Museum of Art, founded in 1924, in adjacent Loch Haven Park.
Local craft breweries include Ivanhoe Park Brewing Co. and Ten10 Brewing Company.
The district takes its name from Lake Ivanhoe on its eastern edge.
w: North of downtown along Lake Ivanhoe runs Antique Row, the heart of a district of vintage shops, vinyl stores, breweries and quirky bars. Less polished than its neighbours, it keeps an offbeat charm and a skyline view across the water. Browse the junk shops, then find a patio. Orlando's retro corner.

hook: A preserved citrus-era brick downtown on Plant Street, where two old railbeds became the West Orange Trail running straight through the main street.
facts: Downtown Winter Garden's Plant Street commercial district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1996.
Plant Street is wide because two railroads ran down its middle — the Orange Belt Railway (1886) and the Tavares & Gulf line (1899).
The surviving downtown is brick because two fires destroyed the original wooden buildings, and citrus shipping wealth funded the rebuild.
In 1994 Orange County removed the Plant Street tracks and laid the West Orange Trail, a 22-mile rail-trail running through downtown.
Plant Street Market, a 12,000-square-foot indoor artisan market anchored by Crooked Can Brewing Company, opened in May 2015.
w: A preserved old citrus town twenty minutes west, where brick-paved Plant Street runs between restored storefronts, a food market and the West Orange Trail. Once a packing hub for the groves, it reinvented itself as a small-town charmer without losing its bones. Pedal in, then linger over lunch. Old Florida, polished but real.
